arguments_against: Strongest counter-hypothesis: with 44 Simmons officials and 9 Simmons biographies in the corpus, and a bio carrying initials only, a different E. B. Simmons could be the Tanganyika judge. This fails: the biography prints a continuous judicial career — 'puisne judge, Maur., 1952; Tang., 1959' — i.e. a puisne judgeship in Tanganyika from 1959, and the staff list shows E. B. Simmons as High Court Judge, Judiciary, Tanganyika in 1960-1961. 'Puisne judge' IS a High Court judge (the position_sim FAIL at 59 vs bar 60 is a pure string artifact; semantically the titles are the same office). The Tanganyika bench was small, with a handful of named judges; two E. B. Simmonses on it simultaneously is not credible. Dates align exactly (appointed 1959, listed 1960-61), and the later bio version's closing note '(Tanzania Govt. service.)' explains why the colonial-list stint ends at 1961 — Tanganyika's independence — rather than indicating a different person. Both initials agree, with 2 co-occurring edition-years.
would_overturn: Evidence of a second E. B. Simmons on the Tanganyika High Court bench in 1960-1961, or a primary source showing the biography subject remained in Mauritius past 1959.
notes: The position_sim FAIL is a string-similarity artifact: a puisne judge is a High Court judge. Place, date, semantic office, initials, and edition co-occurrence all agree; the stint's 1961 end matches Tanganyika independence and the bio's note that he continued in Tanzania government service.

arguments_against: The single-initial trap applies: 'F. Southworth' could in principle be a different F. Southworth among the 3 officials with this surname, e.g. a relative also in colonial service. This fails for several independent reasons: the biography prints a specific judicial career — 'asst. judge, Nyasa., 1953; puisne judge, 1955' — and the staff list shows exactly that progression in exactly that colony (Assistant Judge in 1955, Puisne Judge from 1956 onward), a specific senior-office match, not a generic junior title; there are at most one or two puisne judges in Nyasaland at a time, so a second F. Southworth holding the same bench seat is not plausible; and the bio and stint co-occur in 10 edition-years (1955-1964) with colony-agreeing position matches. The single-initial constraint is satisfied by the specific position match plus multi-edition co-occurrence, not by tenure overlap alone.
would_overturn: Discovery of a second F. Southworth on the Nyasaland bench in 1955-1964, or evidence the biography subject left Nyasaland before 1964 while the staff listing continued under a different person.
notes: Despite the single-initial warning, this is a textbook strong match: the printed entry's 1953 assistant judgeship and 1955 puisne judgeship in Nyasaland are mirrored year-for-year by the staff list, with 10 co-occurring editions. Rare surname (3 officials, 1 biography). The honour FAIL is an artifact: the staff list prints no honours for these rows (bio's Q.C. simply not recorded there).

arguments_against: Strongest counter-hypothesis: a second J. C. Hooton (the corpus has 5 Hooton officials and 2 Hooton biographies), e.g. a relative, holding the Bermuda post. This fails: Attorney-General of Bermuda is a unique, senior office held by one person at a time, and the biography explicitly prints 'atty.-gen., Berm., 1958' — exact office, exact colony, with the staff listing beginning the very next edition year (1959) and ending in 1961, exactly when the bio records 'leg. sec. E.A.C.S.O., 1961', i.e. his departure from Bermuda. Both initials (J, C) agree. The career shape (legal officer: crown counsel 1950-51, senior asst. legal sec. 1953) leads naturally to an attorney-generalship. The honour FAIL is an artifact: the staff list prints no honours in these rows at all. The other Hooton biography is not in this case and no evidence suggests it held this office.
would_overturn: Evidence that a different J. C. Hooton was Bermuda's Attorney-General 1959-1961, or that the biography subject never took up the 1958 appointment.
notes: Unique senior office (Attorney-General, Bermuda) printed verbatim in the biography with matching start (1958 appointment, listed from 1959) and end (1961 move to E.A.C.S.O. legal secretary) plus full initial agreement and 2 co-occurring edition-years.

Forename conflict and profession conflict. The biography is PAVLIDES, Spyros, a physician ('hon. radiologist, Cyp., 1939; med. offr. spec. (radiologist), 1949'). The stint is keyed to 'Pavlides, Stelios', whose records 1936-1952 show a legal career: 'Crown Counsel' (1936, 1939) then 'Attorney-General' (1946-1952, C.M.G.) - a different first name (Stelios vs Spyros) and an entirely different profession. The only deterministic position match (best 85: 'hon. radiologist' ~ 'Medical Officer Specialist (Radiologist)') comes from a single 1951 row printed as 'S. G. Pavlides' in the Research department, which is almost certainly a record of Spyros mis-clustered into the Stelios stint by the shared initial 'S.' (the namesake trap flagged in the dossier). The stint as a unit belongs to Stelios the lawyer, not Spyros the radiologist; assigning it would merge the Attorney-General's career into the doctor's record. No shared honour (Stelios has C.M.G.; the bio lists none). Left unassigned per the no-false-merges directive.

arguments_against: Strongest case against: the stint chain is keyed 'Morgan, H. G. St. G' — the extra 'St. G' suggests a man with initials H. G. St. G. (e.g. 'St. George'), which 'Hywel Glyn' does not supply, so this could be a different H. G. Morgan among the 85 Morgans in the staff lists. Why it fails: every year-by-year row actually prints plain 'H. G. Morgan', so the 'St. G' is an artifact of one fuller listing or OCR, not a stable contradiction; and the office is not generic — Assistant Attorney-General then Attorney-General of Nyasaland is a unique senior post held by one person at a time. The bio dates 'asst. atty.-gen., Nyasaland Prot., Mar., 1932' and 'atty.-gen., Aug., 1936' reproduce exactly the staff-list progression (Assistant Attorney-General 1933-1936 editions, Attorney-General from the 1937 edition, consistent with an Aug. 1936 promotion appearing in the next year's volume). A second H. G. Morgan simultaneously holding the same unique Nyasaland law offices on the same dates is not a realistic counter-hypothesis. Age (b. 1899, aged 34 at stint start) and 1948-edition confirmation ('K.C. (Nyasa. and N. Rhod.)') corroborate.
would_overturn: Evidence of a contemporaneous H. G. St. G. Morgan in the Nyasaland legal department distinct from Hywel Glyn Morgan (e.g. a staff list printing both men in the same year, or a bio for an 'H. G. St. George Morgan'), or a record showing Hywel Glyn Morgan was elsewhere than Nyasaland in 1933-1940.
notes: The chain key's 'St. G' does not appear in any of the printed year rows (all 'H. G. Morgan'); worth checking where the chain name came from, but it does not affect the identification: the promotion from Assistant Attorney-General to Attorney-General of Nyasaland between the 1936 and 1937 editions matches the bio's dated Aug. 1936 appointment exactly.

arguments_against: Strongest case against: 8 officials share this surname (a prominent West Indian family), so a same-initial relative could in principle account for one of the stints, and the Windward Islands stint FAILED every deterministic place/position check. This fails on the verbatim text: the bio explicitly records 'atty.-gen., St. L., 1938; Wind. Is., 1942; puisne judge, Wind. and Leeward Is., 1949; J'ca., 1953' - exactly the progression the Windward Islands stint shows (Attorney-General 1939-1950, then 'Puisne Judge, Windward and Leeward Islands (St. Vincent Circuit)' 1952-53) and exactly when the Jamaica stint begins (Puisne Judge, 1954-56, after the 1953 move to Jamaica). The deterministic FAILs on the Windward stint are a parser artifact: the compiled timeline inherited 'St. Lucia' as the place for the 1942 and 1949 clauses, but the printed text names 'Wind. Is.' and 'Wind. and Leeward Is.' directly; St. Lucia was itself a Windward Islands presidency, explaining the 1939 AG listing. A relative-namesake hypothesis would require a second A. R. Cools-Lartigue holding the identical, singular AG-then-puisne-judge offices in the same years that the biography claims for this man, which is not realistic. The two stints are one continuous career, so the partition is internally consistent.

evidence_cited.fact: puisne judge, Wind. and Leeward Is., 1949
Puisne Judge, Windward and Leeward Islands (St. Vincent Circuit)

would_overturn: Discovery of a second A. R. Cools-Lartigue in the legal service of the Windward Islands or Jamaica in 1939-1956 (e.g. a brother or son with the same initials holding the AG or puisne judge posts), or evidence that the 1950 biography conflated two relatives' careers.
notes: The Windward Islands stint's deterministic FAILs (colony, tenure_overlap, position_sim) are all caused by the timeline parser inheriting 'St. Lucia' as the place for the 1942 'Wind. Is.' and 1949 'puisne judge, Wind. and Leeward Is.' clauses. The verbatim 1950 entry text resolves this unambiguously. Jamaica stint also passes strict edition co-occurrence (1954, 1955, 1956) with position_sim 100.

arguments_against: The strongest case against: the stint name carries only the initial 'C', and the dossier flags the single-initial namesake trap, so a different C. Furness-Smith (a relative, or a successor in the same post) could be the Zanzibar Attorney-General. This fails for several reasons. Attorney-General of Zanzibar is a unique, senior legal office, not a generic junior title that recurs across namesakes; the biography states this exact appointment with a date ('atty.-gen., Zanzibar, Mar., 1936') that immediately precedes the stint's first listing year (1937). The corpus contains only 2 officials with this rare hyphenated surname and a single biography, so the namesake pool is tiny. The stint ends in 1940, exactly when the 1950 biography records him moving on ('atty.-gen., Zanz., 1936; T.T., 1940') — the career shape and the staff-list window agree end-to-end. A successor hypothesis would require another barrister named C. Furness-Smith holding the identical office in the identical years, which the biography already claims for itself; no counter-hypothesis survives.
would_overturn: Evidence of a second contemporaneous barrister named C. Furness-Smith serving in East Africa in the late 1930s, or a staff-list record showing the Zanzibar Attorney-General 1937-1940 with a forename other than Cecil.
notes: The seven OCR variants of the surname (FURNES-, FURNNESS-, FURNESSE-, RNESS-) are clearly the same printed biography; the compiled timeline is internally consistent across all editions. The stint's 1937-1940 listing window matches the biography's Zanzibar tenure exactly, including the 1940 transfer to Tanganyika Territory.

arguments_against: The strongest case against: the Kenya stint prints only 'A. G.' (the C is missing), its rows carry no honours, and the bio says he became 'dep. publ. prosecutor, 1950' while the 1950-51 staff rows still call him Crown Counsel -- so the Kenya man could be a relative (A. G. without C) in the same legal department. This fails. (1) The surname is exceptionally rare: 2 officials and 1 biography in the whole corpus, and the two officials are exactly these two stint name-keys, which the bio's own career spans. (2) The bio claims the precise Kenya office and date the stint records: 'crown coun., Ken., 1949' against 'Crown Counsel' in the LEGAL department in the 1950-51 editions -- a one-edition lag and continued substantive listing while acting as deputy prosecutor is normal staff-list behaviour, not a conflict. (3) For Northern Rhodesia the agreement is total: 'puisne judge, N. Rhod., 1954-60' vs Puisne Judge rows 1956-1960, full A. G. C. initials, and the 1958-59 rows print 'O.B.E., Q.C.' matching the bio's post-nominals exactly, with five agreeing edition-years. (4) The two stints chain into one coherent legal career (Kenya counsel -> Rhodesian judge) exactly as the single biography narrates it. A hypothetical second A. G. Somerhough would have to occupy the very office and years the bio subject documents for himself.
would_overturn: Evidence of a second Somerhough in colonial legal service (e.g. a staff record distinguishing an 'A. G.' from an 'A. G. C.' serving simultaneously in different posts), or an external record showing the bio subject had left Kenya before 1950.
notes: The Kenya rows listing him as Crown Counsel in 1950-51 despite the bio's 1950 promotion to deputy public prosecutor is consistent with edition lag and substantive-vs-acting listing practice. The bio's 'dep. publ. prosecutor, 1950' clause itself confirms continued Kenya legal service through the stint years.

arguments_against: The single-initial trap is flagged: the early bio versions print only 'CONROY, D. W.', and 8 Conroys appear in staff lists, so another D. Conroy in Northern Rhodesia is conceivable. But the counter-hypothesis requires a second D. W. Conroy who was simultaneously Chief Justice of Northern Rhodesia 1961-1964, held the C.M.G., and was knighted in exactly 1962 — the same year the biography records 'Kt. (1962)' and the staff list switches from 'D. W. Conroy' to 'Sir D. W. Conroy'. Chief Justice is a unique office, not a generic junior title, and the bio explicitly states 'ch. just., N. Rhod., 1961–65', matching the stint years 1961-1964. A father/son or namesake cannot occupy the same unique post in the same years. The counter-hypothesis fails on every independent dimension.
would_overturn: Evidence of a second D. W. Conroy serving in the Northern Rhodesia judiciary in 1961-1964, or a record showing Diarmaid Conroy held a different post elsewhere during 1961-1964 incompatible with the Chief Justiceship.
notes: Unique senior office (Chief Justice), exact place and year agreement, shared C.M.G., and knighthood year 1962 independently corroborated by the staff list's switch to 'Sir'. Initials D. W. match the printed bio heading exactly.

arguments_against: 'Extra Office Assistant' is a junior title, which is normally weak. But the full forename 'Robert James' = R.J., colony Ceylon, the 1920 appointment continuing into the 1922-1925 listing, and the entry naming the Colombo Kachcheri extra-office-assistant post all agree. The junior title is the only caveat; name+colony+years carry it.
would_overturn: A second same-surname person in the same colony and years holding the same office, or a printed death/birth date excluding these years.
notes: pilot hand-adjudication (Claude Code, in-session)

arguments_against: The deterministic pass flags that the supporting career events inherit their place from an earlier clause, so a sceptic could argue the Attorney-Generalship was held somewhere other than Uganda, and there is a second Dreschfield in the staff lists who could be a relative. But the inheritance chain starts from an explicitly printed place ('res. mag., Uga., 1938') with no intervening colony change, and both bio versions close with '(Uga. Govt. service.)', so the Uganda location is not actually in doubt. The counter-hypothesis of a second R. L. E. Dreschfield holding the unique office of Attorney-General of Uganda over exactly the years 1951-63 stated in the bio, with the same three initials on a very rare surname (only 2 in the entire corpus) and the same C.M.G., is not realistic. Attorney-General is a singular senior post, not a generic junior title.
would_overturn: Evidence of a second R. L. E. Dreschfield in Uganda government service in the 1950s, or a record placing Ralph Leonard Emmanuel Dreschfield's attorney-generalship in a different territory.
notes: Very rare surname, exact three-initial match, unique senior office with exact tenure agreement (bio 1951-63 vs stint 1952-1962), shared C.M.G. whose 1957 award date is consistent with its appearance in staff lists from 1959. The 1962 'Commissioner on Special Duty' record is a plausible end-of-tenure transition consistent with the bio's atty.-gen. tenure ending 1963.

arguments_against: The strongest counter-hypothesis is a second T. H. Mayers in Kenya (a relative or coincidental namesake), since the corpus has 4 officials with this surname in staff lists. But the biography itself, in its 1962-1964 version, records 'puisne judge, Ken., 1952' and '(Ken. Govt. service.)' -- the exact post, colony, and start year of the stint -- and the stint's 1954 record carries the honour Q.C., matching the bio's Q.C./K.C. A namesake who was ALSO a Q.C. puisne judge in Kenya in exactly 1952-54 while the biography claims that precise post would be an extraordinary coincidence; no other biography with this surname exists in the printed sections to compete for it. The honour check 'FAIL' is mechanical: the bio lists Q.C. (formerly K.C.) and the 1954 staff list shows Q.C., so the honours in fact agree.
would_overturn: Evidence of a second T. H. Mayers serving as a Kenya puisne judge in 1952-1954, e.g. a separate biography or staff-list entry distinguishing two individuals, or a record showing Thomas Henry Mayers remained in Jamaica through 1953-54.
notes: All deterministic checks pass except the mechanical honour check, which misses that K.C. and Q.C. are the same honour renamed at the 1952 accession; the 1954 staff list's Q.C. actually corroborates the bio. Position match is exact (puisne judge, Kenya), date matches the bio's 1952 appointment, and initials T.H. match the full name Thomas Henry.

arguments_against: Strongest case against: Macpherson is very common here (29 officials, 11 biographies), the bio has no printed birth year, position_sim failed mechanically, and the stint contains rows that cannot be this man — 'Lieut.-Col. Macpherson | Accountant | Militia and Defence Department' (1877, 1878) is a serving departmental accountant, not a sitting senator; that is a second individual swept into the cluster by surname. One could extend the doubt to the whole stint. That extension fails for the senator rows: the bio's subject was 'speaker of the senate till 1883' and the staff list shows 'D. L. Macpherson | Speaker of the Senate (without Portfolio)' in the Queen's Privy Council list and 'Speaker' of the Senate in exactly 1883 and no other year — a unique office with exact date agreement; he was 'summoned by royal mandamus to a seat in the senate', matching the continuous Senator listings; and the staff lists acquire 'Sir' and 'K.C.M.G.' from 1886, immediately after the bio's K.C.M.G. (1884). A rival D. L. Macpherson who was simultaneously Senator, Speaker in 1883, Privy Councillor, and K.C.M.G. is not a live hypothesis. Confidence is held at probable solely because the stint cluster is contaminated by the Militia accountant rows and should be split before the link is published.

evidence_cited.fact: Sir D. L. Macpherson | Senator | The Queen's Privy Council for Canada | K.C.M.G.

would_overturn: Evidence of a second David L. Macpherson in the Canadian Senate in this period, or a record showing the Speakership of 1883 was held by someone other than the bio's subject; alternatively, confirmation that the Lieut.-Col. accountant rows are the same man would force a full re-examination.
notes: Identity of the senator rows is effectively beyond doubt (unique 1883 Senate Speakership, Privy Council membership, K.C.M.G. appearing in listings from 1886 after the 1884 award). The verdict is probable rather than certain because the stint cluster is contaminated: the 1877-78 'Lieut.-Col. Macpherson, Accountant, Militia and Defence Department' rows are a different person (no forename printed, incompatible occupation) and should be split out upstream. The bio's 'resigned 1885' refers to leaving the ministry, not the Senate, so the Senator listings through 1896 are consistent.

arguments_against: Strongest counter-hypothesis: Trimingham is a prominent Bermudian family name (6 officials with the surname, 2 biographies), so a relative — even a son with the same initials — could hold a Bermuda post. This fails: Colonial Auditor of Bermuda is a unique, named senior office, and the biography prints 'col. audr., Berm., 1943' — exact office and colony, with the stint (Colonial Auditor, Audit dept., 1949-1952) being the direct continuation of that 1943 appointment; all three initials H. G. L. match the full forenames Harold Gilbert Lutyens; and his entire printed career (audit board member 1932, chairman 1934-43) is in Bermuda's audit establishment, the same department as the stint. A same-initial relative holding the identical unique office during the same years is not a realistic alternative. The honour FAIL is an artifact: neither source prints honours.
would_overturn: Evidence of a second H. G. L. Trimingham serving as Bermuda's Colonial Auditor 1949-1952, or evidence the biography subject retired or died before 1949.
notes: Unique office (Colonial Auditor, Bermuda) printed in the biography from 1943; stint 1949-1952 is its continuation. Three-initial agreement and 3 co-occurring edition-years. Age 57-60 during the stint is consistent with a senior auditor approaching retirement.

arguments_against: Smith is the worst surname in the corpus (612 officials, 265 bios) and the bio carries only the forename 'Allan' with no middle name, so the namesake trap is at full strength. Against the Sierra Leone assignment: the stint prints the middle name 'Chalmers' which the bio never confirms, so it could be a different Allan Smith. This fails because Chief Justice of Sierra Leone is a unique office: the bio says 'ch. just., S.L., 1950' and the stint shows the Chief Justice from 1951, and decisively, the bio's Kt. (1953) is mirrored by the staff list switching from 'A. C. Smith' (1951-52) to 'Sir Allan Chalmers Smith' in exactly 1953. Two knighted Allan Smiths cannot both have been CJ of Sierra Leone in 1953. Against the Bermuda assignment: the chain key carries a middle initial 'F' which conflicts with 'Chalmers'. But not one of the nine printed rows contains an F — they read 'Allan Smith' then 'Sir Allan Smith' — so the F is a chain-keying artifact, not printed evidence. The bio's man was Bermuda-bred ('ed. Warwick Acad., Berm.'), retired there in 1955, and was appointed 'asst. justice, 1956'; the stint is the Assistant Justice of Bermuda from 1957, knighted, exactly continuing that appointment. A second knighted Allan Smith as Bermuda's Assistant Justice in the same years is not a realistic hypothesis.
would_overturn: For Sierra Leone: a record of a different knighted Allan Smith as CJ there in 1951-54. For Bermuda: any printed source actually giving the Assistant Justice a middle initial F or a forename other than Allan, or evidence the bio's subject left Bermuda before 1957.
notes: The Gold Coast chain should be split upstream: its 1949-50 'A. C. Smith, Puisne Judge, Judicial' rows would likely link to this bio cleanly once separated from the agriculture and income-tax records. The Bermuda chain's 'F' middle initial appears in the chain key only, never in any printed row.

Conflated chain mixing at least three distinct people: 'A. C. Smith, Puisne Judge' (1949-50), 'A. Smith, Principal Agricultural Education Officer' (1950), and 'A. H. Smith, Deputy Commissioner, Income Tax' (1951); the chain key 'A. St. L' matches none of them. The Puisne Judge rows are plausibly the biography's subject (bio: 'G.C., 1940' as puisne judge, departing for the Sierra Leone chief justiceship in 1950), but assigning the stint would also attach the agriculture and income-tax officers — people who cannot be him, since he was Chief Justice of Sierra Leone by 1950-51. Under the no-false-merge directive the whole stint stays unassigned; it needs chain repair, not adjudication.

Different person. A. M. MacLeod Smith was Financial Secretary of Sierra Leone 1958-59 with a C.M.G.; the biography's subject was a judge, never a financial officer, held no C.M.G. (his honour was Kt. 1953), and had left Sierra Leone for Bermuda by 1955 ('retd., apptd. conservr.-gen., Berm., 1955'). Initials and honours both conflict.

arguments_against: Williams is the weakest possible surname signal (380 officials, 162 biographies share it) and the bio gives initials only. The strongest counter-hypothesis: the stint chain conflates TWO D. J. Williamses in Bermuda's education service — the bio prints 'inspr., schs., Berm., 1946', yet the staff list shows D. J. Williams as 'Organiser of Physical Education' in 1949-1951 and as Inspector of Schools only from 1953; if the bio subject was already an inspector in 1946, the 1949-51 organiser rows could be a different man. Why I still assign: the chain is a single continuous name in one small colony's Education department with a natural promotion ladder (PE organiser -> Inspector of Schools -> Director of Education), no year shows two D. J. Williamses simultaneously, and the anchor matches are very strong and unique — 'dir., educ., 1956' against 'Director of Education' listed 1957-1966, and 'inspr., schs.' against 'Inspector of Schools' 1953-55 — with 10 co-occurring edition-years. The 1946-vs-1949 title discrepancy is most plausibly bio compression or retitling, but it is a real tension, so this stays below 'certain'.
would_overturn: Evidence of two contemporaneous D. J. Williamses in Bermuda's Education department (e.g. an edition listing both an Organiser of Physical Education and an Inspector of Schools named D. J. Williams), or a primary source showing the bio subject held the inspectorship continuously from 1946 while someone else was the PE organiser.
notes: The Director of Education and Inspector of Schools rows (1953-1966) match the biography decisively. The 1949-1951 'Organiser of Physical Education' rows are the residual uncertainty: the bio claims an inspectorship from 1946, which those rows do not show. If the chain is ever split, the 1953-1966 portion is the safe core.

arguments_against: Smith is the commonest surname in the corpus (612 officials, 265 biographies), so the prior on any name-based match is weak, and one could posit a different P. J. C. Smith in Bermuda. This fails: Solicitor-General of Bermuda is a unique, named law office held by one person at a time; the biography prints 'solr.-gen., Berm., 1952' — exact office, exact colony, appointed the year before the stint begins; all three initials (P, J, C) agree; and bio and stint co-occur in exactly the same two editions (1953, 1954), appearing and disappearing together — the synchronised window itself indicates one person. Two distinct P. J. C. Smiths holding the same unique Bermuda law office in the same two years is not a realistic hypothesis. The honour FAIL is an artifact: neither source prints honours.
would_overturn: Evidence that a different P. J. C. Smith held the Bermuda solicitor-generalship in 1953-1954, or that the biography subject never took up the 1952 appointment.
notes: Despite the maximally common surname, the match rests on a unique senior office (Solicitor-General, Bermuda) printed verbatim in the biography with consistent dates, full three-initial agreement, and perfectly synchronised edition windows (both appear only in 1953-1954).

The stint is a conflation of two distinct persons: Sir Charles Tupper (father; Bart., G.C.M.G., C.B., Minister of Railways and Canals, High Commissioner for Canada in London) and his son Sir Charles Hibbert Tupper (K.C.M.G. 1893, Minister of Marine and Fisheries, the biography's subject). The same edition-years list both men concurrently with incompatible honours — e.g. 1905 has both 'Sir C. H. Tupper ... K.C.M.G., K.C.' and 'Sir C. Tupper, Bart. ... G.C.M.G., C.B.' — and the 1877-1886 rows (Charles Tupper, C.B., Minister of Public Works/Railways, High Commissioner) predate or contradict the bio, which enters the Commons only in 1882 and explicitly calls the subject 'son of late Sir C. Tupper'. Assigning this mixed stint to the son's biography would merge the father's career into his record, a false merge; the stint must be split upstream before any assignment.

arguments_against: Strongest case against the 1917 stint: 'Abrahams' is a moderately common surname (10 officials in the corpus), the deterministic position check FAILED ('town mag.' vs '3rd Magistrate' scored 36), and a same-initialed relative could conceivably be a junior magistrate in Zanzibar while Sidney Solomon held the town magistracy. This fails: the double initial 'S. S.' is far more specific than a single initial; the biography places him in Zanzibar's judiciary continuously through the stint window — 'town mag., Zanzibar, June, 1915' and 'sole edr., Zanzibar law rept., 1918-22' bracket 1917-1919 exactly; both titles are magistracies in the same small Judicial and Legal Departments establishment, and the position FAIL is a fuzzy-string artifact ('town mag.' vs '3rd Magistrate' are plausibly the same bench seat under different naming conventions, and no other career event competes for those years). Two concurrent S. S. Abrahams magistrates in tiny Zanzibar is not a realistic hypothesis. Strongest case against the 1923 stint: a successor of the same name in the Attorney-General's chair. This fails outright: the biography itself claims 'atty.-gen., Zanzibar, June, 1922' and departure to 'atty.-gen., Uganda, Jan., 1925', which brackets the 1923-1924 staff listings perfectly, with 2 colony-agreeing edition co-occurrences and a Zanzibar honour (Order of Brilliant Star of Zanzibar, 1924) awarded during precisely this stint.
would_overturn: Discovery of a second S. S. Abrahams in the Zanzibar judicial establishment in 1917-1919 (e.g. a staff list showing both a town magistrate Abrahams and a 3rd Magistrate Abrahams simultaneously), or a record showing the Zanzibar Attorney-General of 1923-24 with forenames other than Sidney Solomon.
notes: The 1924 honour '2nd cls. Order of Brilliant Star of Zanzibar (1924)' falls inside the 1923-24 Attorney-General stint, further corroborating presence in Zanzibar. The gap between the two stints (1920-1922) is explained by the documented Mesopotamia secondment ('seconded for serv. with Mesopotamia civil admnstr. Jan., 1920').

arguments_against: The case is flagged for the single-initial trap (31 Turnbulls in the staff lists), and the 1954-55 rows print 'R. E. Turnbull' with a middle initial E that the bio (forename 'Roland' only) cannot confirm — so one could posit a different R. E. Turnbull as governor. That hypothesis collapses on the joint evidence: Governor and Commander-in-Chief of North Borneo is a unique office; the bio prints 'gov. and c.-in-c., N. Borneo, 1954-60' and the stint occupies exactly 1954-1957 of that span; the same chain switches to 'Sir Roland Turnbull' in 1956, confirming the forename; and the honours track the bio's C.M.G. (1946) / K.C.M.G. (1956) perfectly — C.M.G. in the 1954-55 rows, K.C.M.G. plus 'Sir' from 1956, the precise award year. A namesake governor would require two Roland/R. E. Turnbulls holding the same unique post in the same years with the same honour transition. The position_sim miss (59 vs bar 60) is a string-abbreviation artifact, not a substantive disagreement.
would_overturn: Evidence that the 1954-55 'R. E. Turnbull' governor was a different person from the 1956-57 'Sir Roland Turnbull' (i.e. a mid-term succession the bio's 1954-60 tenure contradicts), or any record of a second Roland Turnbull in Borneo service.
notes: Unique office, tenure subset of the bio's printed 1954-60, forename confirmed in 1956-57 rows, and the C.M.G.-to-K.C.M.G. transition lands exactly on the bio's 1956 award year. The 'E' middle initial in 1954-55 is additional detail (Roland Evelyn Turnbull) rather than a conflict, since the same chain resolves to 'Sir Roland' upon knighthood.

arguments_against: The strongest case against: 'Black' is a very common surname (60 officials, 29 biographies in the corpus), the bio carries effectively a single forename, and 'Robert Black' could be a namesake -- e.g. an unrelated Sir Robert Black knighted separately who happened to govern Hong Kong while the biography subject served elsewhere. (1) The bio's own career clause reads 'gov. and c.-in-c., S'pore, 1955; H.K., 1958-64' -- an exact match to the stint's title 'Governor and Commander-in-Chief' and to its exact listing range 1958-1964. (2) The honour chronology inside the stint corroborates the bio's honour dates precisely: the staff list shows K.C.M.G. through 1961 and G.C.M.G. from 1962, matching the bio's 'G.C.M.G. (1962), K.C.M.G. (1955)'. (3) The bio also places him in Hong Kong immediately before ('col. sec., H.K., 1952'), giving career-shape continuity. A namesake would have to share the surname, forename, knighthood class, the year of elevation to G.C.M.G., and the governorship years simultaneously -- not a live hypothesis.
would_overturn: Evidence of a second Sir Robert Black with a K.C.M.G. and a 1962 G.C.M.G. active in Hong Kong in 1958-64, or an external record showing the biography subject held a different post during 1958-64.

The C.B. honour in the 1958 staff-list row is not in the bio's honour list, but bios frequently omit non-order honours; it does not constitute a conflict.

arguments_against: The case carries a single-initial warning, and the Windward Islands stint failed every place-based deterministic check, so a sceptic could argue we are gluing a namesake's governorship onto this biography. But the warning is overcautious: the biography prints the full distinctive forename 'Colville' (rare surname, only 1 Deverell biography and 4 staff-list Deverells in the corpus), and the verbatim entry explicitly records both posts: 'gov. and c.in-c., Wind Is., 1955; Maur., 1959-62.' (1955) and K.C.M.G. (1957); the Mauritius lists show Sir Colville (Montgomery) Deverell, K.C.M.G., in 1960-61, matching 'Maur., 1959-62'. would_overturn: Evidence of a second Colville Deverell in colonial service in the 1950s, or records showing the Windward Islands governor of 1955-57 had a forename other than Colville or honours inconsistent with C.M.G. (1955)/K.C.M.G. (1957).

Deverell'. Both governorships are explicitly printed in the bio; the deterministic failures on the Windward stint stem from a place-inheritance parsing error, not from the source text.

arguments_against: Strongest case against: this stint is visibly a conflation of more than one Boyd. The 'John Boyd' rows listed as 'Senator' (1883, 1889, 1890) and the 'J. Boyd' rows as 'Government Agent' (1877-79) carry no middle initial/name and describe offices the biography never mentions; the historical Senator John Boyd of New Brunswick was a different man, and a Chancellor of Ontario does not simultaneously sit as a Senator. If I assign the whole stint, I attribute those rows to the Chancellor — a partial false merge. Why the assignment still stands at stint level: the stint's key name is the full 'Boyd, John Alexander', and the dominant, continuous record (Chancellor, 1883-1915, 'John A. Boyd'/'John Alexander Boyd') matches the biography on every dimension — 'Chancellor of Ontario' vs the bio's 'chan. of Ontario, 1881', the K.C.M.G. carried in the 1913-15 rows matching the bio's honours, departments 'High Court of Justice' matching 'pres. of high ct. of just., 1887', and the 1913-15 rows coinciding with the bio's edition span. The identity of the stint's principal is not in doubt; the doubt is row-level contamination, which caps confidence at probable so a human can strip the Senator/Government Agent rows.
would_overturn: Evidence that the Chancellor rows belong to a different John A. Boyd than the K.C.M.G. biographee (effectively none conceivable), or — more realistically — a decision to split the stint, which would remove the Senator and Government Agent rows from this person without overturning the core identity.
notes: REVIEWER: this stint conflates at least two and possibly three people under the Boyd key. Rows printed 'John A. Boyd'/'John Alexander Boyd' as Chancellor (1883-1915) are securely the biographee (chan. of Ontario 1881, K.C.M.G.). Rows printed 'John Boyd' as Senator (1883, 1889, 1890; Senate of Canada) and 'J. Boyd' as Government Agent (1877-1879) very likely belong to other Boyds (38 share the surname in the staff lists) and should be split out. Confidence held at probable solely because of this row-level contamination; the principal identity match itself is beyond reasonable doubt.

arguments_against: Strongest case against: Fraser is the commonest surname imaginable in this corpus (140 officials, 69 biographies), the biography prints initials only ('J. J.'), no birth year, and the deterministic honour check FAILed — textbook conditions for a namesake trap (two J. J. Frasers in New Brunswick public life, e.g. father and son). The counter-hypothesis fails because the agreement is not a single office but a synchronized career transition within ONE staff-list individual: Attorney-General in the 1879-1880 lists, gone from executive office by 1883 (listed only as 'Member', Legislative Council), then 'Puisne Judge' from the 1886 list onward — exactly tracking the bio's 'Attorney-general and premier, province of New Brunswick... retired, 1882; puisne judge, supreme court, New Brunswick, 1883'. A second J. J. Fraser would have to make the same AG-to-judge move in the same province in the same years. The 1883 row also prints 'Hon. J. J. Fraser, Q.C' matching the bio's 'Q.C.' (the honour FAIL is mechanical — Q.C. was embedded in the name string, not the honours column). Attorney-General and puisne judge of the supreme court are not generic junior titles; they are single-occupancy or near-single-occupancy offices. Edition co-occurrence passes with 5 agreeing edition-years.
would_overturn: Evidence of a second contemporaneous J. J. Fraser who was Attorney-General or a supreme-court judge in New Brunswick in 1879-1894, or proof that the Attorney-General rows and the Puisne Judge rows in this stint belong to two different Frasers rather than one continuous individual.
notes: Single bio, single stint. The biography text was frozen in 1886 and never updated, so it omits the Lieutenant-Governor phase (1894+ rows); the staff list's AG-to-puisne-judge transition matches the bio's 1882/1883 dates with the normal edition lag, and the later Lieutenant-Governor rows are the natural continuation of the same individual (full name 'John James Fraser' printed from 1905). Post-1896 Lieutenant-Governor rows likely reflect retrospective 'Lieutenant-Governors' roll listings.

Lugard' (the staff list prints 'Sir') cannot hold only a C.M.G.; 'C.M.G.' is evidently an OCR/abbreviation slip for K.C.M.G./G.C.M.G., and the remaining honours C.B. and D.S.O. match the bio exactly ('C.B. (1896), D.S.O. (1887)'). (2) The bio states 'gov. of Hong Kong, 1st May, 1907' and the very next clause 'gov., N. and S. Nigeria, 25th Mar., 1912' -- the stint's 1911-1912 listing falls precisely inside that governorship and ends exactly when the bio moves him to Nigeria. arguments_against: Strongest case against: Archibald is a common surname here (16 officials, 12 biographies), the deterministic position check FAILed, and the 1889 staff-list row shows 'M.P.', an office the biography never mentions. (1885)') AND was associated with the Province of Nova Scotia, where the bio's subject was Lieutenant-Governor 1873-83. arguments_against: Strongest case against: a same-name relative (the corpus has 2 Lougheeds in staff lists and 2 biographies), e.g. a son or brother also in Canadian public life, and the deterministic pass found no shared honour. (1916); K.C.' and the staff lists show K.C.M.G. appearing exactly from 1917 (absent 1907-1914) and 'K.C.M.G., K.C.' from 1919 — the award chronology matches precisely, which a relative could not replicate. (1916); K.C.' and the staff lists corroborate the award year (K.C.M.G. first appears in 1917 records). Prot.), 6th Sept., 1907' matching Governor and Commander-in-Chief listed 1908-1909, with identical honours (K.C.M.G., C.B.), and the listing window ends consistent with 'ret., 1910'. arguments_against: The stint is single-initial 'J. Calder' and Calder is common (14 officials, 9 biographies), so the live counter-hypotheses are (a) a different J. Calder in North Borneo, or (b) a successor in the same post after James Calder left. Against (a): Chief Secretary of North Borneo is a unique, senior single-occupant office, and the biography states 'ch. sec., N. Borneo, 1946' with 'O.A.G. periods, 1946-48' immediately before the stint window; the stint holder also carries C.M.G., the bio subject's honour, in every year 1949-1952. Against (b): a successor sharing surname, initial J, AND the C.M.G. in the identical unique post starting the very year the bio (printed in the same 1949-1951 editions) still describes James Calder as the incumbent chief secretary would be an extraordinary coincidence; the bio's continued appearance through 1951 with no successor-shaped career break supports continuity. The link rests on specific position+place agreement plus a shared honour, not on the name.
would_overturn: Staff-list or external evidence that James Calder left North Borneo before 1949 and a different J. Calder, C.M.G., succeeded him as Chief Secretary; or a death/retirement date before 1949.
notes: position_sim FAIL is an abbreviation artifact ('ch. sec.' = 'Chief Secretary'). The O.A.G. (Officer Administering the Government) periods 1946-48 explain why the chief-secretary staff listing may only begin at 1949 in this stint segmentation. Strong independent dimensions per the single-initial rule: exact unique-position+colony match and shared C.M.G. across all four stint years.

arguments_against: The strongest case against: 'Wright' is the commonest surname imaginable in this corpus (159 officials, 63 biographies), the position_sim check formally FAILed (59 vs bar 60), and the 1952 staff row adds a C.B. that the bio never records -- so the Governor could conceivably be a different, decorated Sir A. B. Wright. This fails. (1) The position FAIL is an abbreviation artefact: 'gov. and c.-in-c.' IS 'Governor and Commander-in-Chief'. (2) The staff list spells out the full, distinctive double forename 'Andrew Barkworth', identical to the bio's -- this is not an initials-only collision, and among 63 Wright biographies only this one carries that forename pair. (3) The bio's career ends 'gov. and c.-in-c., Gam., 1947 ; Cyp., 1949', placing him in exactly this unique office from 1949; the stint's 1951-1953 listing falls inside that governorship. (4) The K.C.M.G. matches the bio's 'K.C.M.G. (1948)', correctly pre-dating the stint. (5) Fourteen of the bio's placed events resolve to Cyprus -- his whole formative career (asst. sec. 1922 through col. sec. 1937 and ag. gov. 1938-39) was there, making the return as Governor continuous in shape. The absent C.B. in the bio reflects only that the bio text predates or omits the award; it cannot outweigh a unique-office, full-name, honour-dated match.
would_overturn: Evidence of a second Sir Andrew Barkworth Wright, K.C.M.G., alive in 1951, or an external record showing the 1949 Cyprus governor left before 1951 and was succeeded by a different man of the same full name.
notes: The C.B. appearing in the 1952 staff row is not in the bio's honour list; bios in this series frequently lag or omit non-order honours, and all other dimensions (full double forename, unique office, colony, dates, K.C.M.G. year) agree, so it was not treated as a conflict.

arguments_against: The strongest case against: 'Palmer' is common (61 officials, 25 biographies); the stint's nominal listing range runs to 1946 although the bio subject retired in 1939, which could suggest the later listings belong to a different H. R. Palmer who succeeded to the governorship; and the bio's K.C.M.G. year is inconsistently printed (1935 in one version, 1933 in two others), hinting at sloppy compilation. This fails. (1) The staff list spells out the full forename pair 'Herbert Richmond', identical to the bio -- not an initials collision. (2) The bio's 'gov. and commodr.-in-ch., Cyprus, 1933' matches the stint's unique office 'Governor and Commander-in-Chief' (position_sim 75, PASS), its colony, and its start in the 1934 edition (first edition after a 1933 appointment). (3) The K.C.M.G. in both 1934 and 1936 rows agrees with the bio's knighthood (1933 or 1935 printing -- either way before/at the stint). (4) The only substantive rows are 1934 and 1936, inside the 1933-1939 tenure the bio describes ('ret., 1939'); the 1939/1946 edition listings carry no rows here and are consistent with stale carry-forward, not a second person -- a successor of the same full double name with the same honour is not a realistic hypothesis.
would_overturn: Evidence of a second Sir Herbert Richmond Palmer, K.C.M.G., governing Cyprus in the mid-1930s, or an external record showing the bio subject never took up the 1933 Cyprus appointment.
notes: The bio's two printed versions disagree on the K.C.M.G. year (1935 vs 1933); both predate or coincide with the stint, so the discrepancy does not affect identity. The stint's nominal listing window extends to the 1946 edition while the bio records retirement in 1939; the rows actually shown stop at 1936, so any post-1939 listing should be treated as a stale carry-forward when consolidating tenure dates.

The stint conflates two distinct people, a father and son serving in the same colony at the same time. Most rows (1877-1886) are 'Sir Anthony Musgrave | Captain-General and Governor-in-Chief' with K.C.M.G. — the Governor of Jamaica. Only the 1879 and 1883 rows 'Anthony Musgrave, jun. | Private Secretary' (no honours) belong to the biography subject, who was 'private secretary to governor of Jamaica, Aug., 1877, to June, 1883' and indeed 'private secretary to Sir A. Musgrave' — i.e. private secretary to the other man in this very stint. The biography's printed career begins only in 1932 in the judicial line and mentions no customs or treasury service. Age 18 in 1915 is merely possible, not evidence. With 20 Pace officials in the corpus and a single-initial bio, no specific dimension (position, honour, edition co-occurrence) links this stint; assigning it would rest on surname + initial alone.

arguments_against: Strongest case this is wrong: a same-name relative (e.g., a son or brother also 'G. J. Ransley') could have succeeded to the prisons directorship, or the listing could be an honoured retiree carried after leaving office. Why it fails: the bio states 'dir., prisons, 1944' and the stint shows the singular post 'Director of Prisons' held continuously 1949-1960, exactly the continuation one expects; both initials (G and J) agree, not a single-initial case; the surname is rare (only 3 Ransley officials in the entire staff-list corpus, 1 biography); and the bio and stint co-occur in editions 1955, 1957, 1958 with the matching position (position_sim 78). A successor hypothesis would require the bio's subject to have vacated the post before 1949 with a same-initials namesake taking over — there is no trace of any second G. J. Ransley, and the bio kept being printed with 'dir., prisons' as the current office through 1960, the same years the stint runs. The retiree trap does not apply: the career was current (appointed 1944, aged 49 in 1949).
would_overturn: Discovery of a second G. J. Ransley in Malta (e.g., a son appointed Director of Prisons before 1949), or evidence the biography subject died or left the service before 1949 while the staff-list entries continued under another person.
notes: Single-bio, single-stint case with two-initial agreement, a specific senior position match (Director of Prisons), tenure continuity from the bio's 1944 appointment, and multi-edition co-occurrence (1955, 1957, 1958). The only weak deterministic signal (place_inherited FAIL) reflects the bio's clause-inheritance formatting, not a substantive doubt: the entry's career is entirely Maltese.

arguments_against: Strongest case this is wrong: Zammit is a very common Maltese surname (22 officials in the staff lists, 3 biographies), and Malta's most famous archaeologist-curator family (the Zammits) had multiple members in museum work, so a relative — even a father or uncle 'C. Zammit' — could be the 1936-1940 curator while the bio subject was elsewhere. Why it fails: the bio's career is uniquely specific — 'asst. custodian, archaeol., 1930; custodian, 1934; dir., museum, Malta, 1955' — and the two stints reproduce it exactly in staff-list phrasing: 'Curator of the Archaeological Section' in the Museum department 1936-1940 (the staff-list rendering of 'custodian' of archaeology, printed with BOTH initials 'C. G. Zammit' in 1939-1940, matching 'C. Gr.'), then 'Director of Museum(s)' 1956-1964 continuing the 1955 directorship, with 8 agreeing edition-years (1956-1962) where bio and stint co-occur with matching position (position_sim 75). The dates interlock: custodian from 1934, curator stint opens 1936; director from 1955, director stint opens 1956. A relative-curator hypothesis would require that relative to also carry initials C. G. and hold exactly the post the bio claims for the same years — no such second C. G. Zammit appears.
would_overturn: Evidence of a second C. G. Zammit in the Malta museum service (e.g., a staff list showing two such names concurrently), or a record showing the bio subject was absent from Malta (studying in London) for the whole 1936-1940 curatorship while another C. Zammit held it.
notes: The 1941-1955 gap between the two assigned stints spans the war and the pre-directorship custodian years; the bio records no intermediate appointment, consistent with continuous museum service that the sampled editions simply do not list here.

Wrong leading initial (G. vs the bio's C. Gr.) and an incompatible career: District Medical Officer 1929-1934 then Lyceum schoolmaster 1936-1940. The bio subject, born 1911, would have been an 18-year-old District Medical Officer in 1929 — chronologically impossible for a physician — and was simultaneously the museum's archaeological curator during 1936-1940, so he cannot also be the Lyceum master. This is a different person, plausibly an unrelated G. Zammit.

arguments_against: Strongest case this is wrong: Bird is common in this corpus (34 staff-list officials, 12 biographies), Natal had a well-known Bird family of officials, and the stint's earliest record (1879, 'C. J. Bird', 2nd Clerk, Public Works Department) carries a middle initial 'J' that the bio's plain 'Christopher' does not print, and a department ('Public Works') the bio does not name for 1879 — so the 1879 row could belong to a relative, and the stint chain could be contaminated at its head. Why it fails: from 1883 onward the stint is an unbroken sequence of singular senior offices that mirror the bio clause by clause — 1st Clerk, Colonial Secretary's Department 1883 ('1st cls., Aug., 1881'), Assistant Colonial Secretary 1889-90 ('asst. col. sec., Jan., 1888'), Principal Under Secretary from 1894 ('apptd. prin. under-secr. on establtnt. of responsible govt., Oct., 1893'), Chairman of the Civil Service Board ('chrmn., civ. ser. bd., 1901'), 'Permanent Head of the Civil Service' ('permnt. head of civ. ser., 1903'), and the shared C.M.G. There was exactly one Principal Under Secretary of Natal; two people cannot hold it. Even the 1879 head of the chain fits: the bio has him a clerk in the engineer's department from 1874 (Natal's public-works office) moving to the Colonial Secretary's office in Jan. 1880, exactly when the stint's department changes. The stint ends 1910, matching 'retired, 1911' at the Union. No realistic counter-hypothesis survives.

apptd. prin. under-secr. on establtnt. of responsible govt., Oct., 1893

would_overturn: A staff list showing a second C. Bird in the Natal Colonial Secretary's Office during 1883-1910, or evidence that the 1879 'C. J. Bird' in Public Works is a documented different person (e.g., a distinct biography for a C. J. Bird with a Public Works career), which would split only the 1879 row off the chain, not the 1883-1910 core.
notes: The single-initial warning is mitigated here by the strongest possible independent dimensions: a shared C.M.G., exact matches on multiple unique senior offices (Assistant Colonial Secretary, Principal Under Secretary, Permanent Head of the Civil Service, Chairman Civil Service Board), tenure agreement across three decades, and edition co-occurrence (1883, 1886). The 'J' in the 1879 record is consistent with a middle name the biography simply renders as 'Christopher'.

arguments_against: Strongest case this is wrong: the bio carries a single initial ('A.'), Byrne is common in this corpus (20 staff-list officials, 10 biographies), and the stint prints an extra initial 'X' (1879-1886) that the biography never shows — so the staff-list 'A. X. Byrne', a mere Clerk in the Civil Establishment, could be a different man (a brother or cousin) while the bio's A. Byrne spent 1880-1893 as an itinerant private secretary to successive governors and administrators. Also, 'Clerk' is exactly the generic junior title the instructions warn about, and the mechanical position_sim 100 ('Governor's clerk' ~ 'Clerk') is over-generous. Why the link nonetheless holds on balance: the stint is not a generic clerkship — it is repeatedly 'Clerk, Governor's Office' (1880, 1883, 1889, 1890, 1894), the exact substantive post the bio gives ('Governor's clerk, Natal, Mar., 1878'), opening in 1879 immediately after the bio's appointment; the private-secretary duties in the bio (to officers administering the government, to the governor in 1890, to the administrator in 1893) are precisely the special duties a Governor's Office clerk performs while the staff list keeps printing his substantive post; and the stint's 22-year span (1879-1900) is co-extensive with the bio's edition run (1886-1900), both ending together around 1900. The 'X' is consistent with a middle initial the biography simply omits. A second A. Byrne serving simultaneously as the Governor's Office clerk is not evidenced anywhere in the dossier. The counter-hypothesis is weakened but not impossible, hence probable rather than certain.

evidence_cited.fact: priv. sec. to gov. Natal, Feb., 1890

would_overturn: Any record giving the bio subject's full forename(s) inconsistent with 'A. X.' (e.g., a forename with no X middle name), or a staff list/biography showing a second contemporaneous A. Byrne attached to the Natal Governor's Office or Civil Establishment in 1879-1900.
notes: Sent to human review per 'probable' semantics. Decisive check for a reviewer: Natal civil lists or Blue Books giving the full name of the Governor's Office clerk (is he Alfred Xavier Byrne or similar?), and whether any of the other 9 Byrne biographies claims clerk/Chief Clerk service in the Natal Civil Establishment in the 1890s.

arguments_against: Strongest case this is wrong: the bio is single-initial-equivalent ('George' -> G.) and a son also named George Lamond could have succeeded his father as Registrar of Deeds, with the staff list silently switching persons mid-stint; alternatively, the career started in 1853, so by the late 1890s the man would be elderly and the listing could be a stale carry-over. Why it fails: the office is singular and highly specific — the bio's 'registrar of deeds and distributor of stamps, and registrar-general of births, deaths, and marriages, 1878' is reproduced almost verbatim in the staff list as 'Registrar of Deeds, and Registrar-General of Births, Deaths, and Marriages' from 1879, exactly one year after the bio's appointment; the bio and stint co-occur with this matching position in at least five edition-years (1883, 1886, 1888, 1889, 1890) and run in lockstep through 1900, with the bio's edition run (1883-1900) and the stint's span (1879-1900) ending together; and Lamond is rare — only 2 officials with the surname in the whole staff-list corpus. A succession by a same-initial son would show as a break or re-listing; the record is one continuous tenure in the same singular office the bio claims. The age concern is mild: a man starting as a postmaster in 1853 (say, born ~1830) serving to 1900 at ~70 is unusual but not anomalous for a registrar-general, and the bio kept being reprinted as a current officer through 1900.

evidence_cited.fact: Registrar of Deeds, and Registrar-General of Births, Deaths, and Marriages
registrar of deeds and distributor of stamps, and registrar-general of births, deaths, and marriages, 1878

would_overturn: Evidence of a second G. Lamond in Natal service (e.g., the other Lamond official in the corpus proving to be a same-initial relative holding the deeds registry in part of 1879-1900), or a death/retirement record for George Lamond before 1900 while the staff-list entries continued.

The other Lamond biography in the corpus is not in this case; if it ever claims the Natal deeds registry, this verdict should be revisited.

arguments_against: Strongest counter-case: 'Hunter' is a common surname (64 officials, 37 biographies in the corpus), and the stint runs to 1906 while the biography is only printed in 1883 and 1886 — the later staff-list years (C.M.G. 1899, knighted by 1905) could in principle be a different D. Hunter who succeeded to the same post, with the staff list silently swapping incumbents under the same initial. This fails because the post is singular and highly specific — there is one 'General Manager, Natal Government Railways' at a time — and the staff list shows an unbroken run of the identical name and identical title from 1883 to 1906 with no discontinuity that would mark a succession; the biography's appointment date (27th September, 1879) precedes and is consistent with the stint's first listing in 1883; and the biography co-occurs with the stint in both of its editions (1883, 1886) at position similarity ~100. A namesake holding the very same unique senior post in the same colony in the same years, listed under the same initial, is not a realistic hypothesis. The single-initial trap does not apply with force: the biography carries the full forename DAVID, and the required strong independent dimension (specific unique position match plus multi-edition co-occurrence) is present.

would_overturn: Evidence that the David Hunter of the 1879 appointment left or died before 1883 and a different D. Hunter held the General Managership when the staff lists begin; or a second contemporaneous D. Hunter in the Natal railway department revealed by fuller records.

The post (General Manager, Natal Government Railways) is unique and senior; biography appointment date 1879 fits the stint's 1883 start; edition co-occurrence in both bio editions with exact position agreement. The deterministic honour FAIL is expected: the bio text predates the C.M.G./K.C.M.G. shown in the 1899-1906 staff lists.

The stint conflates two distinct contemporaneous Chadwicks in Natal, so assigning it whole to the biography would be a false merge. The staff lists contain two parallel record streams under this stint key: (a) 'J. Chadwick' in the Telegraph/Postal departments — 'Clerk in Charge' (1880), 'General Sub-Manager' of 'Telegraphs' (1883), 'Postmaster-General and General Manager of Telegraphs' (1886-1894), 'Postmaster-General' (1896-1899) — which matches the biography of CHADWICK, JOHN closely ('gen. sub-man. of tels., Natal, 1880; postmnr.-gen., 1883; postmnr.-gen., and gen. man. of tels., Jan., 1884'); and (b) 'J. C. C. Chadwick' in the Judicial line — 'Registrar' of the 'Judicial Department' (1879, 1880), 'Magistrate' (1896-1900), 'Judge' of the 'Native High Court' (1906-1910). The two streams co-occur in the same edition years with different names and different departments (1880: J. Chadwick / Telegraph Department alongside J. C. C. Chadwick / Judicial Department; 1896, 1898, 1899: J. Chadwick / Postal and Telegraphs alongside J. C. C. Chadwick / Magistrates), proving they are two simultaneous officials, not one career. The biography never mentions any registrar, magistrate, or judicial post, and its initials ('J') do not carry the 'J. C. C.' of the judicial officer. The stint as clustered belongs to at most one person only if it is split upstream; as delivered it mixes both, so it stays unassigned.

arguments_against: Strongest counter-case: the biography carries effectively a single distinguishing initial in the staff lists ('R. Plant'), the stint starts in 1894 while the biography dates the appointment to 1888 (a six-year gap before the first listing), and a relative — for instance a son or brother also named R. Plant — could in principle have served in the same Natal Education Department. This fails for three reasons. First, the post is specific and rare, not a generic junior title: 'Inspector, Native Education' in Natal's Education Department, and the corpus holds only 8 Plants in all staff lists. Second, the career shape matches at a fine grain: the biography records promotion to 'senr. inspir. of native schls., 1st July, 1904', and the staff lists show exactly that transition — 'Inspector, Native Education' through 1900, 'Senior Inspector, Native Education' from 1905 onward. A namesake would have to duplicate both the unusual office and the 1904 promotion. Third, the 1894 start is a listing artifact, not a contradiction: the stint's edition list simply begins at 1894, and an 1888 appointee being listed from 1894 is fully consistent (the bio itself only enters the biographical section in 1907). No second R. Plant appears anywhere in the Natal lists to sustain a rival hypothesis.

senr. inspir. of native schls., 1st July, 1904

would_overturn: Discovery of a second contemporaneous R. Plant in the Natal Education Department; or evidence that Robert Plant left the inspectorship before 1905 and a different R. Plant held the Senior Inspector post.

The decisive evidence is the matched promotion: bio's 1904 elevation to senior inspector is mirrored exactly by the staff lists' shift from 'Inspector, Native Education' (through 1900) to 'Senior Inspector, Native Education' (from 1905). Specific uncommon office + place + initial + promotion-shape + 2-edition co-occurrence; the deterministic honour FAIL reflects only that neither source prints an honour.

arguments_against: The biography carries a single initial ('J') and appears in only one edition, so the namesake trap applies: a second J. Hyslop in Natal (e.g. a relative) could in principle be the staff-list man, and the stint key's initials 'J. H' add a second initial the bio lacks. This fails because the matching dimension is not name-based at all: the bio's printed post, 'Resident surgeon, lunatic asylum, Pietermaritzburg', is a unique, senior, highly specific office, and the staff list shows 'Dr. J. Hyslop' as 'Resident Surgeon' in Natal's Medical Department in the very same 1886 edition, with the stint beginning in 1883 — exactly consistent with the bio's appointment date of 4 July 1882. The 'Dr.' title matches, the colony matches, and the career continues coherently into Medical Superintendent of the Natal Government Asylum. There is no second Hyslop in Natal's medical service to contest the stint (only 3 officials with the surname in the whole graph), so no live counter-hypothesis survives.

notes: Single-initial bio, but the merge rests on an exact, specific senior position match (resident surgeon of the Pietermaritzburg lunatic asylum) with same-edition (1886) colony-agreeing co-occurrence and appointment-date alignment (bio 4 July 1882; stint listed from 1883), not on the shared surname or tenure overlap.

arguments_against: The strongest case against: 12 officials share the surname Nevill in the staff lists, so a same-name relative could conceivably hold a Newfoundland post; and the bio appears in only one edition (1886), so the link rests on one printed entry. This fails because the office is a unique compound title — 'Inspector of lighthouses and superintendent of public buildings' — which matches the staff-list position verbatim, in the same colony, with both initials ('J. T.') agreeing exactly, the bio's appointment date (Jan. 1874) preceding and consistent with the stint's first listing (1877), and a same-edition (1886) co-occurrence at position similarity 100. A colony has exactly one Inspector of Lighthouses and Superintendent of Public Buildings at a time; no second J. T. Nevill exists to contest it.

arguments_against: The strongest case against: 8 officials share the surname Conroy, and the gap between the bio's appointment date (1880) and the stint's first listing (1889) could in theory conceal a succession — a different J. G. Conroy taking over the bench. This fails: the office is a specific senior judicial post ('Central District Court judge') matched verbatim in the same colony, both initials agree exactly, and the bio and stint co-occur in the same editions across more than two decades (1889 through 1915) with colony-agreeing position matches — the biographical entry naming this exact judgeship was reprinted in the very volumes whose Newfoundland staff lists carry J. G. Conroy as Central District Court Judge. A succession by a same-initialled relative while the original's biography continued to be printed unchanged for 25+ years is not a realistic hypothesis; the stint's start at 1889 simply reflects coverage, not appointment.

arguments_against: Two real concerns. (1) The 1917 staff-list row reads 'Member for Placentia' while the biography says 'Elected M.H.A., Fogo, Newfoundland, 1913' — a different district, which could indicate a second Halfyard politician or a row-misalignment/OCR error in the staff list. (2) The stint chain is internally contaminated: in 1921 it contains BOTH 'W. W. Halfyard, Minister of Posts and Telegraphs' AND 'W. Halfyard, 3rd Clerk' in the same year, and a 1927 'W. Halfyard, 2nd Clerk and Accountant'. Two people cannot hold those simultaneously; the single-initial clerk records almost certainly belong to a different W. Halfyard. Why the assignment still stands: the core identification is anchored on the department-head records — 'Colonial Secretary' in 1918 and 1919 matching the bio's 'col. sec., 1918' exactly on office, year, and colony, with both initials W. W. agreeing, plus edition co-occurrence in 1918 and 1919, and corpus context of only one official and one biography with this rare surname. The district discrepancy is outweighed because a sitting member can change districts between 1913 and 1917, and the ministerial offices are unique single-holder posts. The clerk contamination is a chain-construction artifact, not evidence against the bio-to-minister link.

would_overturn: Evidence of a second contemporaneous W. W. Halfyard in Newfoundland politics (e.g. a separate biography or staff-list entry showing a different W. W. Halfyard as Colonial Secretary in 1918), or evidence that the 1918-1919 Colonial Secretary rows belong to someone else and the chain's name field is an OCR conflation.
notes: Confidence held at 'probable' rather than 'certain' for two reasons the reviewer should check: (1) the stint chain appears contaminated — the 1921 list contains both 'W. W. Halfyard, Minister of Posts and Telegraphs' and 'W. Halfyard, 3rd Clerk' in the same year, and the 1927 'W. Halfyard, 2nd Clerk and Accountant' single-initial clerk records likely belong to a different person bundled into this chain upstream; (2) the 1917 row's 'Member for Placentia' conflicts with the bio's Fogo seat. The 1918-1919 Colonial Secretary records themselves are an effectively certain match to the biography.

arguments_against: Strongest counter-case: with 7 Blandford officials in the staff lists and 2 Blandford biographies in the corpus, the family is locally common in Newfoundland, so a same-name relative (e.g. a son or brother also styled S. D.) could in principle hold some of these rows; and the chain is not perfectly clean — it contains 'S. Blandford | Sub-Collector' rows in 1920, 1921 and 1927 that overlap the Sheriff rows and almost certainly belong to a different person folded in upstream. Why it fails: the principal records track the biography across two distinct, unique, single-holder offices over 17 years — Minister of Agriculture and Mines from 1910 (bio: 'M.E.C. and min. of agric. and mines, 1909') through 1917, a Legislative Council listing in 1917 (bio: 'M.L.C. ... 1914'), then Sheriff of Newfoundland from 1918 (bio: 'sheriff of Newfoundland, 1917'). The full forename 'Sydney D.' matches exactly, the birth year 1868 makes him 42 at stint start, and there are 4 agreeing edition-years on the Sheriff office. No relative hypothesis survives a career that switches offices in lockstep with the printed biography.

would_overturn: Evidence of a second contemporaneous Sydney D. Blandford in Newfoundland (e.g. a separate biography with the same name and different career), or evidence that the Minister and Sheriff rows belong to two different men whose names were conflated by the chain builder.

Blandford | Collector' (1927) rows appear alongside the Sheriff rows in the same years and mirror the unassigned 'Blandford, D' customs career; they likely belong to a third Blandford (or to the same customs officer as the D. Blandford chain) and were bundled upstream. The identification of the biography with the Minister/Sheriff records is nonetheless certain. Reviewer may wish to split the customs rows out of the Sydney D chain.

Single-initial 'D. Blandford', Sub-Collector of Customs 1911-1927. This career runs fully concurrent with Sydney D. Blandford's service as Minister of Agriculture and Mines (1910-1917) and Sheriff of Newfoundland (1918-1927); one man cannot hold an outport customs sub-collectorship and those offices simultaneously, and the biography never mentions any customs post (position_sim best 32, no agreeing edition-years). With 7 Blandford officials in the staff lists, this is almost certainly a different person, likely a relative. Initials overlap ('D') plus tenure overlap is exactly the weak evidence the constraints forbid merging on.

The stint conflates two different men. Its 1877-1888 rows ('A. C. Douglas', Secretary, Post Office; 1888 'Postmaster and Secretary to Post Office') match the biography's office verbatim and almost certainly ARE the bio's subject. But the stint is keyed 'Douglas, Adye' and its 1894-1900 rows show 'Adye Douglas' / 'Hon. Adye Douglas' as Member and President of the Legislative Council and Chief Secretary in Cabinet — the well-known Tasmanian politician, a distinct person whose forename is Adye, not 'A. C.'. The name forms never overlap: the Post Office rows are always 'A. C. Douglas', the political rows always 'A. Douglas'/'Adye Douglas', and there is no row tying the postmaster to the Council. With 70 Douglas officials in the corpus, two A.-initial Douglases in Tasmania is entirely plausible. Assigning the whole stint to bio douglas_a-c_e1869 would falsely merge the politician's career into the postmaster's record; the stint should be split upstream before either half is assigned.

arguments_against: Ward is a very common surname (112 officials, 43 biographies), and the stint's first listing (1888) postdates the bio's appointment (1882) by six years, so one could posit a different W. F. Ward in the Analyst's Office. A same-initialled successor whose predecessor's unchanged biography continued in print for 30 years is not a realistic hypothesis; the 1888 stint start reflects staff-list coverage, not appointment.

This stint (Private Secretary / Honorary Private Secretary, Civil Establishment, 1907-1917) starts seven years after both the biography's last printed edition (1900) and the end of the 1878-1900 stint. No bio event overlaps it, no edition co-occurs, and 'Private Secretary' is a generic title. The career shape is suggestive — the bio's subject served repeatedly as private secretary to governors and administrators (1874-1887) and an elderly honorary private secretary would fit a man whose career began in 1863 — but with 80 Brownes in the staff lists and the forename George common, a different (possibly younger) George Browne taking the governor's private-secretaryship in 1907 is a fully live counter-hypothesis. Under the no-false-merge directive this stays unassigned.

arguments_against: Strongest case against: (1) The bio's only specific office is 'sec. for customs, Tasmania, 1894', while the staff list for 1894 prints 'Collector and Landing Surveyor' — Secretary and Collector are not the same office, so the bio could describe a different Barnard heading the customs secretariat while this one collected at the port. (2) The stint chain itself looks conflated: in 1877-1879 it contains two concurrent rows, 'James Barnard, Government Printer' and 'J. Barnard, Warehouse-keeper, Customs Department' — one person cannot hold both rows, and the Government Printer is plausibly an older same-name relative, so part of this stint may not belong to anyone in this dossier. (3) The corpus has 32 Barnard officials and 13 Barnard bios, and the bio is effectively single-initial-grade ('James' is common). Why it still fails to block the link: the customs trajectory in the stint (Warehouse-keeper 1877 -> Senior Landing Waiter 1888 -> Collector and Landing Surveyor exactly in 1894 -> Collector and Inspector to 1909) agrees with a man who 'Ent. govt. service, 1864' and whose one career milestone is a senior customs post in Tasmania dated exactly 1894; the promotion-year agreement (1894), the colony (Tasmania), the department (Customs), the forename/initial, and 4 co-occurring editions (1905-1908) are multiple independent dimensions all pointing the same way. The Secretary/Collector title gap and the Government-Printer contamination keep this below 'certain'.
would_overturn: A staff list or bio showing a separate 'Secretary for Customs' Barnard in Tasmania around 1894 distinct from the Collector; or evidence that the bio's James Barnard served in a non-customs department in 1877-1909; or a birth/death date making the 1864 entry or the 1909 listing chronologically impossible for the customs man.
notes: The stint chain appears internally conflated: 1877-1879 contain two concurrent rows (James Barnard, Government Printer, Miscellaneous Offices vs J. Barnard, Warehouse-keeper, Customs Department). The Government Printer rows likely belong to a different (probably older, same-name) James Barnard; the customs rows are the ones matching this biography. Recommend splitting the chain before publication; the 'probable' confidence applies to the customs career, not the Government Printer rows.
